strengths.concept_label: Basic Risk: Maximum loss on a long options position
strengths.description: Correctly identifies that buying a call option caps the maximum loss to the premium paid — demonstrating a clear understanding that long options are defined-risk instruments, distinguishing them from short positions or direct stock ownership where losses can be far greater.

strengths.concept_label: Covered Calls: Income generation from existing holdings
strengths.description: Correctly selects selling 2 covered calls at the $190 strike against 200 existing Apple shares — showing practical command of covered call sizing (1 contract per 100 shares) and the income-generation rationale of selling upside potential at a pre-defined target price.

strengths.concept_label: Time Decay (Theta): How options lose value over time
strengths.description: Correctly attributes options value erosion as expiry approaches to Theta — demonstrating awareness that options have a time-based cost component independent of directional price movement, a foundational concept for managing both long and short positions.

strengths.concept_label: Portfolio Protection: Using puts to hedge downside risk
strengths.description: Correctly identifies protective puts as the optimal hedge for a volatile tech portfolio — showing understanding that options allow investors to stay invested while capping downside, rather than resorting to panic-selling or doubling down on losing positions.

weaknesses.concept_label: Delta: Option price sensitivity to underlying movement
weaknesses.misconception: Chose 'Volatility sensitivity' for Delta, which is actually the definition of Vega. This Greek misattribution suggests the investor's mental model of options pricing conflates two distinct sensitivities — directional exposure (Delta) and implied volatility exposure (Vega) — which will cause difficulty when interpreting position risk or constructing hedges.

weaknesses.concept_label: Assignment Mechanics: What happens when ITM options expire
weaknesses.misconception: Chose 'You'll lose money' for a covered call expiring in-the-money — indicating a failure to recognise that selling shares at the strike price ($320) plus keeping the $3 premium is actually the intended, profitable outcome of the strategy. This misconception could lead to premature and unnecessary position closures.

weaknesses.concept_label: Cash-Secured Puts: Strategic entry at target price with premium income
weaknesses.misconception: Chose passive waiting ('wait for the stock to hit $400') instead of selling cash-secured puts — revealing an unawareness that puts can generate premium income while creating an obligation to buy at the desired target price. This conflates patience as a virtue with a missed opportunity to be compensated for being willing to buy.

Chose 'Only change if earnings are good' — indicating no mental model of implied volatility as an independent, event-driven component of option pricing. This gap means the investor may be surprised by elevated option premiums before earnings and cannot yet assess whether those premiums are fair value for the anticipated move.

weaknesses.concept_label: Expiration Management: Actively managing ITM positions near expiry
weaknesses.misconception: Chose 'Let them all expire automatically' for 5 ITM positions expiring next week — treating automatic assignment as a safe, passive default rather than recognising that simultaneous assignments across multiple positions can create unintended portfolio concentrations, capital requirements, or tax events requiring active management.

weaknesses.concept_label: Gamma: Accelerating delta sensitivity near expiry
weaknesses.misconception: Chose 'You're protected against volatility' for high gamma — directly inverting the concept. High gamma means the position's delta (and therefore its value) will change rapidly with even small moves in the underlying, making the position more volatile and harder to hedge, not safer.

weaknesses.concept_label: Multi-Leg Strategies: Iron condor mechanics and profit conditions
weaknesses.misconception: Chose 'SPY only goes up' for the iron condor profit condition — misreading a range-bound, premium-collection structure as a directional bullish trade. This reveals no familiarity with how the simultaneous call spread and put spread legs define a bounded profit zone on both sides.

weaknesses.concept_label: Volatility Crush: Post-earnings IV collapse and its impact on options
weaknesses.misconception: Chose 'Time decay acceleration' to explain a 30% overnight option loss on a 5% stock drop — attributing the loss to Theta rather than IV crush. This gap means the investor may underestimate how sharply long options positions can lose value immediately after a binary event even when the directional move is modest.
